summary.hypothesis: Introducing aromatic residues at position 5 and hydrophobic residues at position 11 will enhance complementarity with SSTR2-specific ECL2/TM5 contacts while avoiding off-target interactions. A negatively charged residue at position 6 will disrupt electrostatic interactions with off-target subtypes.
summary.critic_hypothesis: The current design strategy must incorporate explicit selectivity optimization, particularly by targeting non-pharmacophore positions to disrupt off-target interactions.

summary.hypothesis: Mutations at positions 5 and 11 will enhance SSTR2-specific interactions by introducing aromatic residues at position 5 (to complement SSTR2 ECL2 residues 192/193/195/197) and hydrophobic residues at position 11 (to avoid steric clashes with SSTR3/5 TM6 residues 272/273/276/279). This should increase the selectivity margin by differentiating SSTR2 from off-targets.
summary.critic_hypothesis: The hypothesis is updated to prioritize mutations at non-pharmacophore positions to disrupt conserved off-target interactions in SSTR3/SSTR5, while maintaining the pharmacophore for SSTR2 binding.

summary.hypothesis: Introducing aromatic substitutions at position 5 and hydrophobic substitutions at position 11 will enhance complementarity with SSTR2-specific ECL2/ECL3/TM5/TM6 regions, improving selectivity margin over off-target subtypes.
summary.critic_hypothesis: The current hypothesis is that SSTR2 selectivity can be achieved by optimizing non-pharmacophore positions to disrupt off-target interactions. The next iteration will focus on these positions with guided mutations to improve selectivity.

summary.hypothesis: Mutating position 5 to aromatic residues (W/Y) will enhance SSTR2-specific interactions with ECL2/ECL3, while mutating position 11 to hydrophobic residues (L/I/V) will reduce off-target SSTR3/5 binding by avoiding unfavorable electrostatic interactions.
summary.critic_hypothesis: The current design strategy prioritizes SSTR2 affinity but fails to address off-target interactions. A revised hypothesis is that disrupting conserved pocket interactions at non-pharmacophore positions will improve SSTR2 selectivity without compromising binding affinity.
